Name: task_specific_compression_bounds
Title: Fundamental Limits of Task-Specific Compression: A Rate-Distortion Perspective
Short Hypothesis: The minimal bit-cost for compressing a model while preserving task-specific performance can be quantified using a rate-distortion framework, where the distortion measure is explicitly tied to task-relevant information loss.
Related Work: Existing work on model compression (e.g., EMQ, AMP-ViT) focuses on practical quantization methods but lacks theoretical grounding in task-specific information preservation. Rate-distortion theory has been applied to generalization bounds (e.g., Sefidgaran et al., 2022) and lossy compression (e.g., Theis et al., 2022), but not to task-specific compression limits. This proposal bridges this gap by defining a distortion measure based on task-relevant information loss and deriving achievable bounds.
Abstract: Model compression is a critical challenge in machine learning, but existing methods often lack theoretical grounding in task-specific information preservation. This paper proposes a novel framework to quantify the fundamental limits of task-specific compression using rate-distortion theory. We define a distortion measure that captures task-relevant information loss and derive achievable bounds on the minimal bit-cost required to preserve task performance. Our approach connects compression to the information bottleneck principle, quantifying redundancy in representations and providing a theoretical foundation for task-specific quantization and pruning. Experiments on vision and language tasks validate our bounds, showing that practical methods can approach these limits. This work advances the understanding of task-specific compression and provides a principled basis for future compression algorithms.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Derive achievable bounds for task-specific compression on synthetic data with known information-theoretic properties (e.g., Gaussian mixtures). Use mutual information estimators (e.g., InfoNCE) to quantify task-relevant information and validate bounds.

Experiment 2: Apply the framework to vision tasks (e.g., ImageNet classification) and language tasks (e.g., GLUE benchmarks). Quantize models using task-specific distortion measures and compare achieved compression rates to theoretical bounds.
Experiment 3: Evaluate redundancy in representations by comparing the description length of task-relevant features to total model size. Use PAC-Bayes bounds to connect compression to generalization.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Defining task-specific distortion measures may be challenging for complex tasks.
Mutual information estimation can be computationally expensive for high-dimensional data.
Theoretical bounds may be loose for non-i.i.d. data distributions.
Practical quantization methods may not fully exploit the theoretical limits due to hardware constraints.
